hero.title: 🧠 Physiology
hero.subtitle: The intuitive mind is a sacred gift and the rational mind is a faithful servant - Albert Einstein

topics.content:
• Endocrine vs Exocrine: Endocrine = hormones into blood (distant effect); Exocrine = ducts → local action (enzymes, sweat).
  • Hormone Types: Peptides (water-soluble), Steroids (cholesterol-derived, lipid-soluble), Amines (mixed behavior).
  • Water-Soluble Hormones: Stored in vesicles, circulate free, bind cell surface receptors → act via 2nd messengers (FAST).
  • Lipid-Soluble Hormones: Synthesized on demand, travel bound to proteins, bind intracellular receptors → alter gene transcription (SLOW).
  • Peptide Hormone Synthesis: Preprohormone → Prohormone (ER/Golgi) → Stored in vesicles → Released by exocytosis.
  • Steroid Hormones: Derived from cholesterol (4-ring structure) → diffuse across membranes → no storage.
  • Transport: Water-soluble = free in plasma; Steroids/thyroid = bound to carrier proteins (inactive reservoir, longer half-life).
  • Half-Life: Peptides = short (minutes); Steroids/thyroid = long (hours–days).
  • Receptors: Water-soluble = membrane receptors; Lipid-soluble = intracellular (cytoplasm/nucleus).
  • Negative Feedback: Free hormone levels regulate secretion → maintains homeostasis.
  • Major Glands: Hypothalamus, Pituitary (master), Thyroid, Parathyroid, Adrenal, Pancreas, Gonads.
  • Functions: Regulates metabolism, growth, reproduction, blood pressure, electrolytes, behavior.

• Pituitary Location: Lies in the sella turcica at the base of the brain and is connected to the hypothalamus via the infundibulum (pituitary stalk).
  • Divisions: Anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis = glandular), Posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis = neural), Pars intermedia (minimal in humans).
  • Embryology: Anterior pituitary develops from Rathke’s pouch (oral ectoderm); Posterior pituitary develops from neuroectoderm of the hypothalamus.
  • Posterior Pituitary: Stores and releases ADH and Oxytocin (produced in hypothalamus) via axonal transport (magnocellular neurons).
  • ADH Function: Increases water reabsorption in kidneys → concentrates urine.
  • Oxytocin Function: Causes uterine contraction and milk ejection (let-down reflex).
  • Anterior Pituitary Control: Regulated by hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones via the hypothalamic–hypophyseal portal system.
  • Portal System: Specialized vascular system connecting hypothalamus to anterior pituitary for rapid hormone delivery.
  • Hypothalamic Hormones: TRH (↑TSH), CRH (↑ACTH), GHRH (↑GH), Somatostatin (↓GH), GnRH (↑FSH/LH), Dopamine (↓Prolactin).
  • Dopamine: Key inhibitor of prolactin secretion (Prolactin-Inhibiting Hormone).
  • Anterior Pituitary Cells: Somatotropes (GH), Corticotropes (ACTH), Thyrotropes (TSH), Gonadotropes (FSH/LH), Lactotropes (Prolactin).
  • Most Abundant Cells: Somatotropes (~30–40% of anterior pituitary).
  • Growth Hormone (GH): Acts via JAK-STAT pathway and through IGF-1 (somatomedin) to promote growth.
  • GH Effects: Increases cell size, mitosis, protein synthesis, and bone growth.
  • GH Disorders: Excess in children → Gigantism; Excess in adults → Acromegaly; Deficiency → Dwarfism.
  • Posterior vs Anterior: Posterior = neural control & hormone storage; Anterior = endocrine gland regulated by blood-borne signals.
  • Median Eminence: Interface where hypothalamic hormones enter portal circulation.
  • Key Concept: Hypothalamus controls pituitary → pituitary controls peripheral endocrine glands (master regulator system).

• Pancreatic Islets: Clusters of endocrine cells (Islets of Langerhans) responsible for glucose homeostasis.
  • Cell Types: Alpha (glucagon), Beta (insulin, amylin), Delta (somatostatin), PP cells (pancreatic polypeptide).
  • Distribution: Beta cells (~60–80%) central; Alpha cells (~20–30%) peripheral.
  • Insulin Structure: Peptide hormone with A and B chains connected by disulfide bonds; derived from proinsulin.
  • C-Peptide: Released in equimolar amounts with insulin; used to assess endogenous insulin production.
  • Insulin Storage: Stored in granules as a crystalline complex with zinc.
  • Insulin Secretion Trigger: Increased blood glucose → ↑ ATP → closure of ATP-sensitive K+ channels → depolarization → Ca2+ influx → exocytosis.
  • Sulfonylureas: Stimulate insulin release by blocking ATP-sensitive K+ channels.
  • Insulin Receptor: Tyrosine kinase receptor with α (binding) and β (kinase) subunits.
  • Mechanism of Action: Autophosphorylation → activation of IRS → metabolic and gene transcription effects.
  • Immediate Effects: GLUT4 translocation → ↑ glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue.
  • Intermediate Effects: Enzyme phosphorylation → metabolic changes.
  • Long-Term Effects: Gene transcription → protein synthesis and growth.
  • GLUT Transporters: GLUT4 (insulin-dependent), GLUT2 (pancreas/liver sensor), GLUT1/3 (brain/RBCs, insulin-independent).
  • Insulin Effects on Carbohydrates: ↑ glucose uptake, ↑ glycogenesis, ↓ gluconeogenesis.
  • Insulin Effects on Lipids: ↑ lipogenesis, ↓ lipolysis, ↓ ketogenesis.
  • Insulin Effects on Protein: ↑ amino acid uptake, ↑ protein synthesis, ↓ proteolysis.
  • Electrolyte Effects: ↑ K+, Mg2+, phosphate uptake into cells.
  • Hormone of Plenty: Insulin is secreted in the fed state to promote storage of nutrients.
  • Regulation: Stimulated by glucose, amino acids, incretins (GLP-1, GIP), parasympathetic activity; inhibited by sympathetic activity.
  • Glucagon: Secreted by alpha cells; acts via cAMP to increase blood glucose.
  • Glucagon Effects: ↑ glycogenolysis, ↑ gluconeogenesis, ↑ lipolysis.
  • Target Organ: Liver is the primary target for glucagon.
  • Insulin vs Glucagon: Insulin lowers blood glucose; glucagon raises it.
  • Fed State: ↑ insulin, ↓ glucagon → storage of nutrients.
  • Fasting State: ↓ insulin, ↑ glucagon → mobilization of energy stores.
  • Insulinoma: High insulin + high C-peptide → endogenous overproduction.
  • Exogenous Insulin Use: High insulin + low C-peptide.
  • Disulfide Bonds: Essential for insulin activity; disruption leads to inactive hormone.
  • Growth Effects: Insulin promotes IGF-1 production and works synergistically with GH.
  • Fetal Effects: Insulin deficiency → low birth weight; excess → macrosomia.
  • Antagonistic Hormones: GH and cortisol oppose insulin effects.
  • Key Concept: Insulin is the main anabolic hormone that promotes storage, while glucagon is catabolic and mobilizes energy.

1_intro.q: Which of the following best describes an endocrine gland?
a: Secretes substances through ducts to a surface
Releases hormones directly into the bloodstream
Acts only locally at site of release
1_intro.l: Endocrine glands release hormones directly into the bloodstream to act on distant targets.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is an example of an exocrine function of the pancreas?
1_intro.l: The exocrine pancreas secretes digestive enzymes into ducts leading to the intestine.

1_intro.q: Which hormone type is most likely stored in secretory vesicles?
1_intro.l: Water-soluble peptide hormones are synthesized and stored in vesicles for rapid release.

1_intro.q: Which hormone type is synthesized on demand rather than stored?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and synthesized when needed from cholesterol.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is TRUE about water-soluble hormones?
1_intro.l: Water-soluble hormones bind surface receptors and act via second messenger systems.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is TRUE about lipid-soluble hormones?
a: They act via cell surface receptors
1_intro.l: Lipid-soluble hormones enter cells and alter gene transcription, leading to protein synthesis.

1_intro.q: Which hormone class includes thyroid hormones?
1_intro.l: Thyroid hormones are amines but behave like lipid-soluble hormones.

1_intro.q: Where are peptide hormones synthesized?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones are synthesized in the rough ER as preprohormones.

1_intro.q: What is the first step in peptide hormone synthesis?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones begin as preprohormones before being processed into prohormones.

1_intro.q: Which organelle converts preprohormone to prohormone?
1_intro.l: The ER cleaves preprohormone into prohormone.

1_intro.q: Where is prohormone packaged into vesicles?
1_intro.l: The Golgi apparatus packages prohormones into secretory vesicles.

1_intro.q: What triggers peptide hormone release?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones are released via exocytosis when vesicles fuse with the membrane.

1_intro.q: Which of the following hormones is derived from cholesterol?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones like cortisol are synthesized from cholesterol.

1_intro.q: Which gland produces cortisol?
1_intro.l: Cortisol is produced by the adrenal cortex.

1_intro.q: Which structure is part of steroid hormone backbone?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones share a four-ring cholesterol backbone.

1_intro.q: Which hormone travels mostly bound in plasma?
1_intro.l: Thyroid hormone is mostly bound to carrier proteins in blood.

1_intro.q: What is the function of carrier proteins?
1_intro.l: Carrier proteins increase hormone stability and prolong half-life.

1_intro.q: Which hormone has the shortest half-life?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones have short half-lives (minutes).

1_intro.q: Where do lipid-soluble hormones bind receptors?
1_intro.l: Lipid-soluble hormones bind intracellular receptors.

1_intro.q: Which system works with endocrine system to maintain homeostasis?
1_intro.l: Endocrine and nervous systems coordinate to regulate homeostasis.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is regulated by hormones?
1_intro.l: Hormones regulate metabolism, reproduction, blood pressure, and more.

1_intro.q: Which gland is considered the master endocrine gland?
1_intro.l: The pituitary gland controls other endocrine glands.

1_intro.q: Which hormone is produced in the pancreas?
1_intro.l: The pancreas produces insulin from beta cells.

1_intro.q: What is C-peptide?
1_intro.l: C-peptide is released when proinsulin is converted into insulin.

1_intro.q: What maintains hormone levels in the body?
1_intro.l: Negative feedback regulates hormone levels to maintain balance.

1_intro.q: Which form of hormone provides negative feedback?
1_intro.l: The free (active) hormone level controls feedback mechanisms.

1_intro.q: Which of the following describes hormone action speed?
1_intro.l: Peptides act fast via second messengers; steroids act slowly via gene transcription.

1_intro.q: Which gland produces aldosterone?
1_intro.l: Aldosterone is a steroid hormone produced by the adrenal cortex.

1_intro.q: Which of the following hormones can diffuse through cell membranes?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and diffuse through membranes.

1_intro.q: Which of the following best describes endocrine signaling?
1_intro.l: Endocrine hormones travel through blood to distant target organs.

2_pituitary_gland.q: The pituitary gland is located in which anatomical structure?
2_pituitary_gland.l: The pituitary sits in the sella turcica at the base of the brain.

2_pituitary_gland.q: The anterior pituitary develops from which embryologic structure?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Anterior pituitary arises from Rathke’s pouch (oral ectoderm).

2_pituitary_gland.q: The posterior pituitary is derived from:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Posterior pituitary originates as neural outgrowth from hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which cell type is found in the posterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Posterior pituitary contains glial-like cells called pituicytes.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which of the following hormones is produced in the hypothalamus but released from the posterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland.l: ADH is synthesized in hypothalamus and released from posterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Oxytocin primarily acts on which organs?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Oxytocin causes uterine contraction and milk ejection.

2_pituitary_gland.q: ADH primarily affects which organ?
2_pituitary_gland.l: ADH increases water reabsorption in kidneys.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which neurons synthesize ADH and oxytocin?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Magnocellular neurons synthesize ADH and oxytocin.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Hormones of the posterior pituitary reach the gland via:
2_pituitary_gland.l: They travel via axons from hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which system regulates the anterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Anterior pituitary is regulated by hypothalamic portal system.

2_pituitary_gland.q: The hypothalamic–hypophyseal portal system connects:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Portal system carries releasing hormones to anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which hormone inhibits prolactin release?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Dopamine acts as prolactin-inhibiting hormone (PIH).

2_pituitary_gland.q: GHRH stimulates secretion of:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Growth hormone releasing hormone stimulates GH release.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Somatostatin primarily inhibits:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Somatostatin inhibits GH release.

2_pituitary_gland.q: CRH stimulates release of:
2_pituitary_gland.l: CRH stimulates ACTH from anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland.q: GnRH stimulates secretion of:
2_pituitary_gland.l: GnRH stimulates FSH and LH.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which anterior pituitary cells secrete GH?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Somatotropes secrete growth hormone.

2_pituitary_gland.q: ACTH is secreted by which cells?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Corticotropes secrete ACTH.

2_pituitary_gland.q: TSH is secreted by:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Thyrotropes produce TSH.

2_pituitary_gland.q: FSH and LH are secreted by:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Gonadotropes produce FSH and LH.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Prolactin is secreted by:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Lactotropes produce prolactin.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which anterior pituitary cells are most abundant?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Somatotropes make up ~30–40% of anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Growth hormone acts primarily via:
2_pituitary_gland.l: GH signals via JAK2-STAT pathway.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which mediator carries out many effects of GH?
2_pituitary_gland.l: IGF-1 mediates GH effects.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Growth hormone primarily promotes:
2_pituitary_gland.l: GH increases cell size, mitosis, and differentiation.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which condition results from GH excess in childhood?
2_pituitary_gland.l: GH excess before epiphyseal closure → gigantism.

2_pituitary_gland.q: GH excess in adults leads to:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Acromegaly occurs after epiphyseal closure.

2_pituitary_gland.q: GH deficiency in childhood results in:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Low GH → short stature (dwarfism).

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which structure connects hypothalamus to pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Infundibulum (pituitary stalk) connects them.

2_pituitary_gland.q: The median eminence is part of:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Median eminence links hypothalamus to pituitary portal system.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Anterior pituitary secretion is controlled by:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Controlled by hypothalamic hormones via portal system.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Posterior pituitary secretion is controlled by:
2_pituitary_gland.l: Posterior pituitary is controlled by nerve signals.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which hormone increases water reabsorption in kidneys?
2_pituitary_gland.l: ADH increases water reabsorption in collecting ducts.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which hormone causes milk ejection reflex?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Oxytocin causes milk ejection.

2_pituitary_gland.q: Which hormone is responsible for milk production?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Prolactin stimulates milk production.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A nursing mother notes that her milk begins to flow just moments after she hears her infant cry in the next room, even before the baby begins to suckle. This milk ejection reflex is mediated by oxytocin causing the contraction of which of the following cells?
a: Smooth muscle of the lactiferous ducts
2_pituitary_gland.l: Oxytocin causes contraction of myoepithelial cells to eject milk.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A 68-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after three days of severe vomiting and diarrhea. On examination, he is lethargic and has a blood pressure of 80/45 mmHg and a heart rate of 125 bpm. Laboratory studies reveal that his plasma osmolarity is slightly lower than normal due to the ingestion of large amounts of plain water during his illness. Despite the low osmolarity, his plasma antidiuretic hormone (ADH) levels are found to be 40 times the normal reference range. Which of the following best explains why ADH remains elevated despite the patient's hypo-osmolar state?
a: Activation of osmoreceptors in the organum vasculosum.
Decreased stretch of baroreceptors overriding osmotic inhibition.
Increased sensitivity of the V2 receptors in the collecting ducts.
Direct stimulation of the supraoptic nuclei by atrial natriuretic peptide.
2_pituitary_gland.l: Severe hypovolemia activates baroreceptors which overrides osmotic control of ADH.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A researcher is investigating the growth-promoting effects of growth hormone (GH) in a canine model. In one experimental group, the animals have undergone a total pancreatectomy. Despite receiving daily injections of high-dose recombinant growth hormone, these animals show no significant increase in lean body mass or bone growth. Based on the physiological requirements of GH, which of the following is most likely responsible for the lack of growth in these animals?
a: Deficiency of the JAK2-STAT signaling pathway.
Excessive mobilization of free fatty acids causing ketosis.
Reduced half-life of growth hormone in the absence of pancreatic enzymes.
2_pituitary_gland.l: GH requires insulin for IGF-1 mediated growth; without insulin, growth is impaired.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A researcher is studying the development of the endocrine system. They identify a structure that invaginates from the pharyngeal epithelium and eventually loses its connection to the oral cavity. This structure, known as Rathke's pouch, will eventually give rise to which of the following?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Rathke’s pouch forms the anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis).

2_pituitary_gland.q: A 45-year-old patient suffers a traumatic injury to the median eminence of the hypothalamus. While the posterior pituitary function remains partially intact due to the proximity of neuronal cell bodies, the regulation of the anterior pituitary is severely compromised. Which of the following delivery mechanisms for hypothalamic factors is primarily impaired in this patient?
a: Axoplasmic flow via the hypothalamic-hypophysial tract
Direct neural stimulation of glandular cells
2_pituitary_gland.l: Anterior pituitary hormones are regulated via hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal circulation.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A 48-year-old male with an acidophilic adenoma of the anterior pituitary presents with fatigue and increased thirst. Laboratory tests reveal a fasting blood glucose of 145 mg/dL. Which of the following best describes the metabolic effect of his condition on glucose utilization?
a: Increased glucose uptake in skeletal muscle
2_pituitary_gland.l: Excess GH decreases glucose uptake and increases blood glucose → insulin resistance.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A clinical study investigates factors that inhibit the release of GH. It is found that a specific hypothalamic hormone exerts a powerful inhibitory effect on somatotropes. Which of the following substances primarily inhibits GH secretion by blocking the action of Growth Hormone-Releasing Hormone (GHRH)?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Somatostatin inhibits GH release from the anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A 52-year-old patient presents with a protruding lower jaw (prognathism), enlarged hands requiring size 14 gloves, and a hunched back (kyphosis). If these symptoms resulted from an acidophilic tumor, which of the following is most likely true regarding the onset of the excess hormone secretion?
a: It began before the fusion of the long bone epiphyses.
It began after the fusion of the long bone epiphyses.
It was caused by a deficiency in IGF-1.
2_pituitary_gland.l: Acromegaly occurs after epiphyseal plate closure.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A patient with central diabetes insipidus is administered a synthetic analog of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) to reduce their excessive urine output. This medication acts on V2 receptors to increase the water permeability of the collecting ducts by stimulating the insertion of which of the following?
2_pituitary_gland.l: ADH inserts aquaporin-2 channels in collecting ducts to reabsorb water.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A patient is brought to the emergency room following a major motor vehicle accident with an estimated blood loss of 25% of their total volume. Which of the following is the most potent driver of the resulting high levels of ADH secretion in this patient?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Hypovolemia is the strongest stimulus for ADH release.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A 32-year-old woman experienced severe circulatory shock due to massive hemorrhage during the delivery of her child. Several months later, she reports an inability to lactate, loss of libido, and significant lethargy. What is the most likely underlying cause of her panhypopituitarism?
2_pituitary_gland.l: Sheehan syndrome is caused by ischemic necrosis of the pituitary due to postpartum hemorrhage.

2_pituitary_gland.q: A group of children is found to have significantly short stature despite having normal or even elevated plasma concentrations of growth hormone. This condition, seen in Laron syndrome, is most likely due to which of the following?
a: Generalized deficiency of anterior pituitary secretion
Inability to form insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1)
2_pituitary_gland.l: Laron syndrome is due to GH receptor defect leading to decreased IGF-1 production.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following cell types in the pancreatic islets primarily secretes insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Beta cells of the pancreatic islets secrete insulin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following hormones is secreted by alpha cells of the pancreas?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Alpha cells secrete glucagon.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following pancreatic cells secretes somatostatin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Delta cells secrete somatostatin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: C-peptide is clinically useful because it reflects which of the following?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: C-peptide is released in equimolar amounts with insulin and reflects endogenous insulin production.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: A patient presents with hypoglycemia. Labs show high insulin and low C-peptide. What is the most likely cause?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Low C-peptide with high insulin indicates exogenous insulin use.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: A patient has high insulin and high C-peptide levels during hypoglycemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: High insulin and C-peptide suggests endogenous overproduction (insulinoma).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Reduction of disulfide bonds in insulin would result in which of the following?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Disulfide bonds are essential for insulin structure and activity.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which glucose transporter is used by pancreatic beta cells to sense glucose levels?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLUT2 is a low-affinity, high-capacity transporter used in beta cells.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following transporters is insulin-dependent?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLUT4 is insulin-dependent and found in muscle and adipose tissue.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following tissues does NOT require insulin for glucose uptake?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Brain uses GLUT1/3 and is insulin-independent.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: What is the primary intracellular event that triggers insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: ATP closes K+ channels leading to depolarization and insulin release.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Sulfonylurea drugs stimulate insulin secretion by acting on which channel?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Sulfonylureas close ATP-sensitive K+ channels, increasing insulin release.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which ion influx directly triggers insulin exocytosis?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Calcium influx triggers vesicle exocytosis of insulin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which receptor type does insulin bind to?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin binds to a receptor with intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which subunit of the insulin receptor has tyrosine kinase activity?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Beta subunit has intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Binding of insulin to its receptor leads to which immediate intracellular event?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin binding triggers receptor autophosphorylation.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Insulin promotes glucose uptake in muscle cells by increasing which transporter?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin increases GLUT4 translocation to the membrane.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following is a major metabolic effect of insulin on the liver?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes glycogen synthesis in the liver.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following best describes insulin’s effect on fat metabolism?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin inhibits lipolysis and promotes fat storage.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following is a major protein metabolism effect of insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes protein synthesis and inhibits breakdown.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which electrolyte is driven into cells by insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes potassium uptake into cells.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which hormone has effects antagonistic to insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Growth hormone antagonizes insulin effects.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which state is characterized by increased insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin increases after meals (fed state).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which incretin hormone enhances insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLP-1 increases insulin secretion.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which nervous system input increases insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Parasympathetic stimulation increases insulin secretion.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which condition inhibits insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Sympathetic stimulation via epinephrine inhibits insulin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which hormone is secreted during hypoglycemia?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Glucagon increases during hypoglycemia.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: What is the main target organ of glucagon?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Glucagon primarily acts on the liver.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Glucagon exerts its effects via which signaling pathway?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Glucagon acts via Gs → cAMP pathway.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which hormone is known as a "hormone of plenty"?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin is released when nutrients are abundant.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which effect occurs within seconds of insulin binding?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin rapidly inserts GLUT4 transporters into membranes.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which effect of insulin occurs over hours to days?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Long-term insulin effects include gene transcription.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which condition results from insulin deficiency in fetus?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin deficiency leads to low birth weight.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which condition results from hyperinsulinemia in fetus?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Excess insulin causes fetal overgrowth (macrosomia).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which enzyme activity is inhibited by insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin inhibits hormone-sensitive lipase, reducing lipolysis.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which process is stimulated by insulin in adipose tissue?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes fat storage (lipogenesis).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which transporter is found in RBCs?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: RBCs use GLUT1 for glucose uptake.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which transporter is high affinity and found in brain?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLUT3 is high-affinity and used in brain tissue.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following increases insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Amino acids stimulate insulin secretion.
